Author: Aniket Sahoo
Title: The Risks of "Vibe Coding" with AI Tools | Aniket Sahoo posted on the topic | LinkedIn
Text:
𝗩𝗶𝗯𝗲 𝗖𝗼𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗜𝘀 𝗖𝗼𝗼𝗹 — 𝗨𝗻𝘁𝗶𝗹 𝗜𝘁’𝘀 𝗡𝗼𝘁 ⚡️ With all these new AI tools out there, building something has never been easier. You can go from idea to MVP in days — sometimes hours — with tools like V0, Bolt, Cursor, and more. But I’ve been thinking about something lately: just because you can build fast doesn’t mean you’re building right ⚠️. I saw a post today about an app (Tea App) that recently left its database wide open. Thousands of users’ data exposed — not because of bad intentions, but probably because basic principles around access control or data privacy weren’t followed 🔓. And I think we will see more of this as more people start building with AI. It’s amazing that non-technical folks can now create real products — but if you don’t understand things like protecting user data, managing roles and permissions, or securing your APIs, things can go wrong fast. I’ve been using Cursor for a personal side project, and it’s honestly been super helpful. Tasks that would’ve taken an hour, I now get done in minutes. Things like transforming data objects or cleaning up boilerplate — AI kills it there ⚙️. But I still review the code, make tweaks, and make sure it fits into what I’m trying to build. That’s where the line needs to be drawn: AI is a tool 🛠️, not a replacement for understanding. I don’t think “vibe coding” is a bad thing. Learning how to use these tools well will be a major skill in the next few years📈. But if you’re relying on AI to write 100% of your product, and not thinking about what’s happening under the hood, you might run into issues you didn’t see coming. We’re not at the point where human developers can be taken out of the loop. Good engineering still matters — not just to ship, but to ship something safe, scalable, and trustworthy ✅. That’s just my two cents.

Great observation about human in the loop to review AI generated code from harmful risk exposure. Considering AI learns from what it is fed with, there is a risk to AI models from deliberate bad information floated from malicious sources. Expertise to examine AI generated code with a sharper lense is a good skill to have. Keep writing Aniket Sahoo
